publications.editedComment: Two seas, one helm. In `svg-regions` mode, the page renders underlay, controlled region fills, optional decoration and line art, selected-region rings, and `regionClick` events without mutating the parent’s fill map. In `raster-flood` mode, it loads a cross-origin base image, replays store-owned fill operations, converts screen clicks to image coordinates, and emits `fillRequest`. Appended operations apply incrementally; undo or reset triggers a full replay from saved base pixels. `exportImage` produces a PNG from either engine. Missing artwork gets a warning rather than a fabricated page. The captain steers; the parent owns the logbook.

reviewer.name: The Caretaker of the Living Soil

publications.editedComment: The canvas accepts growth from outside rather than burying state inside itself. SVG fills come from the supplied region-to-color map, and region clicks merely request a change. Raster fills likewise arrive as replayable operations owned by the parent; the Component keeps a clean base image, applies new operations incrementally, and rebuilds from the base when the sequence shrinks. Selected SVG regions receive visible dashed outlines. The palette resolver converts stored color ids into actual values for display and export. If page art is incomplete, the visitor sees a warning. Healthy soil remembers every layer while leaving cultivation decisions with the larger garden.

This editor creates a recoverable draft, not final game objects. An existing manifest is deep-copied before editing; a new one starts with schema version, draft visibility, free pricing, and one blank location item. Title can generate the pack slug until the id is touched. An optional model scaffold replaces the local draft for review, but saves nothing automatically. Items may be locations, genres, characters, or rewards, with type-compatible shapes and separate title, description, art prompt, and generation prompt fields. Save backfills missing item ids and writes only through `packStore.savePackManifest`, then emits the pack id. Item generation and database persistence remain later checkpoints.

A pack begins as a small universe held in a local manifest. The editor can deep-copy an existing pack or create a draft with title, slug, description, price hook, and item list. A written premise may be sent to the Packmaker scaffold generator; the returned manifest fills the form but still waits for review and an explicit Save. Each item chooses a location, genre, character, or reward type, an allowed storage shape, and its own descriptive and generation prompts. Titles keep item ids synchronized, while an untouched new pack id follows the pack title. Saving writes the manifest only. The stars inside it are generated later, one by one.

Psst—the wrapper tells you what is real if you read the seams. `ProjectFrontPage` receives the Packmaker fallback configuration and places `PackmakerAdminPanel` in its interactive slot. The public pitch describes bundling characters, art, scenarios, and rewards, plus eventual access and sharing controls. The roadmap is less secretive: only the pack concept and Builder placement are marked done; the builder UI and access-control/install flow remain next. The actual panel may provide current administrative tools, but this page itself does not implement pack installation or permissions. It is the labeled doorway, not every room behind the bundle.

Exhibit orientation: this Component is a ProjectFrontPage configuration for Packmaker with an embedded PackmakerAdminPanel. Its destination is the Builder channel’s packs tab. The interpretive text promises reusable bundles of characters, art, scenarios, and rewards, and describes future sharing terms. The deliverables identify the current boundary: pack concept and Builder placement are complete, while a broader pack-builder interface and access-control/install flow are pending. Accordingly, this wrapper presents project context and mounts the existing administrative surface; it does not itself define manifest editing, generation, installation, or permission enforcement. Please proceed to the interactive panel for the implemented controls.

The board grants ideas a small, private place to survive. Its storage key combines project slug and entry noun, so proposals, prompts, or assignments remain separated. A visitor can add a title, a body, or both; edit either field later; cancel an edit; or remove the entry. A deterministic counter-based id avoids Date and Random dependencies. Deep watching persists the list to localStorage, while failed reads or writes leave the current session usable in memory. No backend receives these drafts and no collaborator sees them automatically. The grace here is modest but real: imperfect local thoughts may be revised without pretending they have become project records.

This is a local propagation tray for project ideas. The caller supplies the project slug, entry noun, title, icon, placeholders, and empty-state copy. New entries accept a title and body, receive deterministic ids, and are inserted at the top. Existing entries can be edited or removed. The reactive list is serialized under `project-board:{slug}:{noun}`; changing that key loads the matching bed. Storage exceptions are caught, so private mode or quota pressure leaves drafts alive only in memory instead of breaking the board. There is no API or shared database here. Useful seedlings, clearly labeled as browser-local until someone transplants them.

Scope declaration: this page wraps an implemented `RulerHookedGame` in a ProjectFrontPage and supplies the project’s public description. The pitch describes a fishing and seaside-kingdom slideshow simulation where catches affect later decisions. Its two sections are Cast & catch and Rule the realm. The roadmap marks core-loop design and slideshow structure as done, while playable slideshow UI and catch/kingdom balancing remain listed as next—even though the interactive slot now mounts a game Component. Therefore this file should be read as project framing plus delegation, not as the fishing rules or balance implementation. Those behaviors belong to `RulerHookedGame` and must be evaluated there.
